metadata.title: Structured vs Unstructured Pruning: Measure the Lie

beats.narration_text: Hello. This is Liam, in for Bear. 80 percent of the weights are zero and the model runs at exactly the same speed. Sparse on paper, dense in silicon.

props.command: claude "write prune_benchmark.py: dense baseline vs 50% unstructured (scattered zeros, dense kernel) vs 50% structured (channels removed). Report storage, activation SRAM, MAC count for each."
props.segment: Structured vs Unstructured Pruning: Measure the Lie

beats.narration_text: The book's pruning-rate versus accuracy chart. Our benchmark script sweeps this curve automatically — finding the knee point where structured pruning starts costing accuracy.

beats.act: MANIM — Dense baseline: three metric bars
beats.narration_text: Start with the dense baseline. Three metrics: storage — all the weights. Activation SRAM — the memory needed for intermediate tensors. Latency — the actual inference time. All three set to 100 percent.

beats.act: MANIM — Unstructured 50%: storage drops, latency unchanged
beats.narration_text: Unstructured 50 percent pruning: set half the weight values to zero, scattered randomly. Storage drops to 50 percent — a sparse representation can save bytes. But SRAM stays at 100 percent — activations don't change. And latency stays at 100 percent — the kernel still executes dense matrix multiplications on zeroed weights. The hardware doesn't know they're zero.

beats.act: MANIM — Structured 50%: all three bars drop
beats.narration_text: Structured 50 percent pruning: remove entire channels. Storage drops to 50 percent. SRAM drops to 50 percent — fewer channels mean fewer activations. And latency drops to 50 percent — the kernel is now genuinely smaller. All three bars move. That is the difference between paper sparsity and silicon speedup.

Your turn. Ask Claude: write prune_benchmark.py — dense baseline versus 50 percent unstructured pruning with scattered zeros in a dense kernel versus 50 percent structured pruning with channels removed. Report storage, activation SRAM, and MAC count for each. Run it and see the numbers prove the point. Then add a hypothetical sparse-kernel column showing what unstructured pruning would buy if a sparse kernel existed — that is the gap between paper and silicon.

props.command: claude "write prune_benchmark.py: dense baseline vs 50% unstructured (scattered zeros, dense kernel) vs 50% structured (channels removed). Report storage, activation SRAM, MAC count for each."

props.title: Structured vs Unstructured Pruning: Measure the Lie
